description: This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves and corresponding SEM fractography for two low-density medium-Mn steels (M1: Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C and M2: Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C) in hot-rolled (HR) and intercritical annealed (IA) conditions. Panels (a) and (d) display tensile curves: HR specimens (black curves) exhibit high ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of approximately 1712 MPa (M1) and 1055 MPa (M2) with limited total elongation (TE) of ~14.8% and ~15.4%, respectively, indicating brittle behavior. In contrast, IA specimens (red curves) show reduced UTS (~867 MPa for M1, ~698 MPa for M2) but dramatically enhanced ductility (TE ~71.8% for M1, ~59.4% for M2). Panels (b-c) and (e-f) reveal fracture mechanisms: HR samples display brittle cleavage features including Flat Facets (M1-HR, b) and River Markings (M2-HR, e), while IA samples exhibit ductile Dimple Rupture (c, f). The results demonstrate that IA treatment transforms the failure mode from brittle cleavage (associated with martensite in HR condition) to ductile fracture via the TRIP effect from retained austenite, achieving superior strength-ductility combinations (PSE of 61 GPa% for M1-IA versus 41 GPa% for M2-IA).
